name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayah

```arabic
ٱللَّهُ لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ ٱلْحَىُّ ٱلْقَيُّومُ ۚ لَا تَأْخُذُهُۥ سِنَةٌ وَلَا نَوْمٌ ۚ لَّهُۥ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَمَا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ ۗ مَن ذَا ٱلَّذِى يَشْفَعُ عِندَهُۥٓ إِلَّا بِإِذْنِهِۦ ۚ يَعْلَمُ مَا بَيْنَ أَيْدِيهِمْ وَمَا خَلْفَهُمْ ۖ وَلَا يُحِيطُونَ بِشَىْءٍ مِّنْ عِلْمِهِۦٓ إِلَّا بِمَا شَآءَ ۚ وَسِعَ كُرْسِيُّهُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضَ ۖ وَلَا يَـُٔودُهُۥ حِفْظُهُمَا ۚ وَهُوَ ٱلْعَلِىُّ ٱلْعَظِيمُ (٢٥٥)
```

```english
Allah—there is no god except Him—is the Living One, the All-sustainer. Neither drowsiness befalls Him nor sleep. To Him belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is on the earth. Who is it that may intercede with Him except with His permission? He knows what is before them and what is behind them, and they do not comprehend anything of His knowledge except what He wishes. His seat embraces the heavens and the earth and He is not wearied by their preservation, and He is the All-exalted, the All-supreme. (255)
```

## (Commentary)

**His saying, the Exalted:** الله لا إِله إِلا هو الحي القيوم (Allah! There is no god except Him, the Living, the All-Sustainer). Some discussion regarding the Majestic Name (لفظ الجلالة) has already preceded in *Surah al-Hamd*, noting that whether it is derived from *alaha al-rajul* (أله الرجل) in the sense of being bewildered and enamored, or from *alaha* (أله) in the sense of worship (عبد), its necessary meaning—by way of allusion—is the Essence that encompasses all attributes of perfection.

Some discussion has also preceded regarding His saying, the Exalted: لا إِله إِلا هو (There is no god except Him), under His saying, the Exalted: **وَإِلَٰهُكُمْ إِلَٰهٌ وَاحِدٌ** — "Your God is the One God" (al-Baqarah: 163). Although the pronoun "He" (هو) refers back to the Majestic Name (اسم الجلالة), the Majestic Name—having become a proper noun by predominance (علماً بالغلبة)—denotes the Essence itself insofar as it is the Essence, even if it includes certain descriptive meanings alluded to by the definite article or by its absolute usage. Thus, His saying: لا اله الا هو, denotes the negation of the right of subsistence (حق الثبوت) for any deities affirmed besides Allah.

As for the name "the Living" (الحي), its meaning is the Possessor of Life (ذو الحياة) that is established, following the pattern of other intensive adjectives (الصفات المشبهة) in their indication of permanence and stability.

In their initial observation of the state of existing things, people found them to be of two types: a type whose state does not change to the senses as long as its existence remains established, such as stones and other inanimate objects (الجمادات); and a type whose state may change, and whose powers and actions may be suspended while its existence remains as it was to the senses, such as humans and other types of animals and plants. We often find their powers, senses, and actions suspended, followed by gradual decay (الفساد). Through this, man became convinced that beyond the senses lies another matter which is the principle (المبدأ) for sensations, intellectual perceptions (الادراكات العلمية), and actions based on knowledge and will.

This is what is called "life" (الحياة), and its cessation is called "death" (الموت). Thus, life is a mode of existence (نحو وجود) from which knowledge (العلم) and power (القدرة) emanate.

Allah, the Glorified, has mentioned this life in His speech by way of affirming it. He, the Exalted, said: **اعْلَمُوا أَنَّ اللَّهَ يُحْيِي الْأَرْضَ بَعْدَ مَوْتِهَا** — "Know that Allah revives the earth after its death" (al-Hadid: 17). And He, the Exalted, said: **أَنَّكَ تَرَى الْأَرْضَ خَاشِعَةً فَإِذَا أَنزَلْنَا عَلَيْهَا الْمَاءَ اهْتَزَّتْ وَرَبَتْ إِنَّ الَّذِي أَحْيَاهَا لَمُحْيِي الْمَوْتَىٰ** — "And among His signs is that you see the earth desolate, but when We send down water upon it, it stirs and swells. Indeed He who revives it will revive the dead" (Fussilat: 39). And He, the Exalted, said: **وَمَا يَسْتَوِي الْأَحْيَاءُ وَلَا الْأَمْوَاتُ** — "The living and the dead are not equal" (Fatir: 22). And He, the Exalted, said: **وَجَعَلْنَا مِنَ الْمَاءِ كُلَّ شَيْءٍ حَيٍّ** — "And We made every living thing out of water" (al-Anbiya: 30). These verses encompass the life of all categories of living beings, including humans, animals, and plants.

The same applies to the various types of life. He, the Exalted, said: **وَرَضُوا بِالْحَيَاةِ الدُّنْيَا وَاطْمَأَنُّوا بِهَا** — "and are pleased with the life of this world and satisfied with it" (Yunus: 7). And He, the Exalted, said: **رَبَّنَا أَمَتَّنَا اثْنَتَيْنِ وَأَحْيَيْتَنَا اثْنَتَيْنِ** — "Our Lord! Twice did You make us die, and twice did You give us life" (al-Mu’min: 11). The two instances of giving life mentioned here encompass two lives: one being the life of the intermediate realm (الحياة البرزخية) — *the state between death and resurrection* — and the second being the life of the Hereafter (الحياة الآخرة). Thus, life has categories just as living beings have categories.

Despite affirming this worldly life (الحياة الدنيا), Allah, the Glorified, characterizes it in many places in His speech as something base and insignificant, not worthy of concern. For instance, His saying, the Exalted: **وَمَا الْحَيَاةُ الدُّنْيَا فِي الْآخِرَةِ إِلَّا مَتَاعٌ** — "The life of this world is nothing but a fleeting provision compared to the Hereafter" (al-Ra'd: 26). And His saying, the Exalted: **تَبْتَغُونَ عَرَضَ الْحَيَاةِ الدُّنْيَا** — "seeking the transitory wares of the life of this world" (al-Nisa: 94). And His saying, the Exalted: **تُرِيدُ زِينَةَ الْحَيَاةِ الدُّنْيَا** — "desiring the glitter of the life of this world" (al-Kahf: 28). And His saying, the Exalted: **وَمَا الْحَيَاةُ الدُّنْيَا إِلَّا لَعِبٌ وَلَهْوٌ** — "The life of this world is nothing but play and diversion" (al-An'am: 32). And His saying, the Exalted: **وَمَا الْحَيَاةُ الدُّنْيَا إِلَّا مَتَاعُ الْغُرُورِ** — "The life of this world is nothing but the wares of delusion" (al-Hadid: 20).

He described the worldly life with these attributes, calling it "fleeting provision" (متاعاً), and provision is that which is sought for the sake of something else. He called it "transitory wares" (عرضاً), and wares are what appear and then vanish. He called it "glitter" (زينة), and glitter is the beauty added to a thing so that the thing may be sought for its sake; thus, what occurs is other than what was intended, and what was intended is other than what occurred. He called it "diversion" (لهواً), and diversion is what distracts and occupies you with itself away from what is important to you. He called it "play" (لعباً), and play is the action performed for an imaginary rather than a real end. He called it "the wares of delusion" (متاع الغرور), which is that by which a person is deceived.

All these verses are explained and clarified by His saying, the Exalted: **وَمَا هَٰذِهِ الْحَيَاةُ الدُّنْيَا إِلَّا لَهْوٌ وَلَعِبٌ وَإِنَّ الدَّارَ الْآخِرَةَ لَهِيَ الْحَيَوَانُ لَوْ كَانُوا يَعْلَمُونَ** — "The life of this world is nothing but diversion and play, but the abode of the Hereafter is indeed Life (الحيوان), had they known" (al-Ankabut: 64). This demonstrates that the reality of life—meaning its perfection—is negated from the worldly life in contrast to the reality and perfection of life affirmed for the Hereafter. It is the life after which there is no death. He, the Exalted, said: **آمِنِينَ لَا يَذُوقُونَ فِيهَا الْمَوْتَ إِلَّا الْمَوْتَةَ الْأُولَىٰ** — "secure, they will not taste death therein, except for the first death" (al-Dukhan: 56). And He, the Exalted, said: **لَهُم مَّا يَشَاءُونَ فِيهَا وَلَدَيْنَا مَزِيدٌ** — "There they will have whatever they wish, and with Us there is yet more" (Qaf: 35). Thus, in their life in the Hereafter, they are not overtaken by death, nor are they beset by any deficiency in living or distress. However, the first of the two descriptions—namely, security (الأمن)—is the true characteristic essential to life.

Therefore, the life of the Hereafter is life in reality (بحسب الحقيقة) because of the impossibility of death occurring to it, unlike the worldly life. Nevertheless, Allah, the Glorified, has informed in many other verses that He is the Bestower (المفيض) of the true life of the Hereafter and the One who revives man in the Hereafter, and in His hand are the reins of all affairs. This indicates that the life of the Hereafter is also owned (مملوكة), not an owner (مالكة), and is subjugated (مسخرة), not absolute; meaning it only possesses its aforementioned characteristic through Allah, not by itself.

From here it becomes clear that true life (الحياة الحقيقية) must be such that it is impossible for death to occur to it by its very essence (لذاتها). This cannot be conceived except by life being the very essence (عين ذات) of the living being, not something accidental (عارضة) to it or occurring upon it through the ownership and bestowal of another. He, the Exalted, said: **وَتَوَكَّلْ عَلَى الْحَيِّ الَّذِي لَا يَمُوتُ** — "And put your trust in the Living One who does not die" (al-Furqan: 58). Accordingly, true life is the Necessary Life (الحياة الواجبة), which is His existence being such that He knows and has power by His very essence (بالذات).

From this, it is known that the restriction (القصر) in His saying, the Exalted: **هُوَ الْحَيُّ لَا إِلَٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ** — "He is the Living One, there is no god except Him" (al-Mu’min: 65), is a real restriction (قصر حقيقي), not a relative one (إضافي). The reality of life that is not tainted by death, nor beset by annihilation or cessation, is His life, the Exalted.

Thus, what is most appropriate in our current context regarding His saying, the Exalted: الله لا إِله إِلا هو الحي القيوم, and similarly in His saying, the Exalted: **الم اللَّهُ لَا إِلَٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ الْحَيُّ الْقَيُّومُ** — "Alif, Lam, Mim. Allah, there is no god except Him, the Living, the All-Sustainer" (Al 'Imran: 1-2), is that the word "the Living" (الحي) be a predicate after a predicate (خبر بعد خبر), thereby signifying restriction; for the underlying meaning is "Allah is the Living One." Thus, the verse signifies that life belongs purely to Allah, except for what He bestows upon others.

As for the name "the All-Sustainer" (القيوم), it is said to be on the pattern of *fay'ul* (فيعول), like *qiyam* (القيام) on the pattern of *fi'al* (فيال), derived from *al-qiyam* (القيام). It is a description denoting intensification (المبالغة). *Al-qiyam* is the preservation of a thing, its action, its management (تدبيره), its nurturing (تربيته), its oversight (المراقبة عليه), and power over it. All of this is derived from *al-qiyam* in the sense of standing upright (الانتصاب), due to the customary correlation between standing upright and each of these meanings.

Allah, the Exalted, has affirmed the principle of sustaining (القيام) the affairs of His creation for Himself in His speech, where He, the Exalted, said: **أَفَمَنْ هُوَ قَائِمٌ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ نَفْسٍ بِمَا كَسَبَتْ** — "Is He who is the sustainer of every soul [and watchful] of what it earns [like the gods of the polytheists]?" (al-Ra'd: 33). And He, the Exalted, said—which is more comprehensive than the previous verse: **شَهدَ اللَّهُ أَنَّهُ لَا إِلَٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ وَالْمَلَائِكَةُ وَأُولُو الْعِلْمِ قَائِمًا بِالْقِسْطِ لَا إِلَٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ الْعَزِيزُ الْحَكِيمُ** — "Allah bears witness that there is no god but Him—and [so do] the angels and those who possess knowledge—maintainer of justice, there is no god but Him, the All-mighty, the All-wise" (Al 'Imran: 18). Thus, He informed that He is the Sustainer (قائم) over existing things with justice (بالعدل); He does not give or withhold anything in existence—and existence is nothing but giving and withholding—except with justice, by giving everything what it deserves. Then He explained that this sustaining with justice is the requirement of His two noble names: the All-mighty (العزيز) and the All-wise (الحكيم). By His might, He sustains everything, and by His wisdom, He acts justly within it.

In short, since He, the Exalted, is the Principle (المبدء) from which the existence of everything, its attributes, and its effects originate, and there is no principle other than Him except that it terminates in Him, He is the Sustainer (القائم) over everything from every aspect with the reality of sustaining that is untainted by languor or defect. This belongs to no one else at all except by His permission in some manner. Thus, for Him, the Exalted, there is only sustaining without weakness or languor, and for others, there is only being sustained by Him. Here, there are two restrictions: the restriction of sustaining to Him, and His restriction to sustaining. The first of these two restrictions is indicated by "the All-Sustainer" (القيوم) being a predicate after a predicate for Allah (Allah is the All-Sustainer). The second restriction is indicated by the following sentence, namely His saying: لا تأخذه سنة ولا نوم (Neither slumber nor sleep overtakes Him).

It has become clear from this explanation that the name "the All-Sustainer" (القيوم) is the mother of all the relative names (الأسماء الإضافية) affirmed for Him, the Exalted. These are the names that denote meanings external to the Essence in some way, such as the Creator (الخالق), the Provider (الرازق), the Originator (المبدئ), the Restorer (المعيد), the Giver of Life (المحيي), the Bringer of Death (المميت), the All-Forgiving (الغفور), the Most Merciful (الرحيم), the All-Loving (الودود), and others.

**His saying, the Exalted:** لا تأخذه سنة ولا نوم (Neither slumber nor sleep overtakes Him). Slumber (السنة), with a *kasrah* on the *sin*, is the languor that overtakes an animal at the onset of sleep. Sleep (النوم) is the stagnation (الركود) that overtakes the senses of an animal due to natural factors occurring in its body. A dream (الرؤيا) is something else; it is what the sleeper witnesses in his sleep.

An objection has been raised regarding His saying: سنة ولا نوم (slumber nor sleep), that it is contrary to the order required by eloquence (البلاغة). For the context is one of progression (الترقي), and progression in affirmation is from the weaker to the stronger, such as saying: "So-and-so can carry ten *mans*, nay, twenty," or "So-and-so gives hundreds, nay, thousands." In negation, it is the opposite, as we say: "So-and-so cannot carry twenty, nor even ten," or "He does not give thousands, nor even hundreds." Thus, it should have been said: "Neither sleep nor slumber overtakes Him."

The answer is that the aforementioned order does not always revolve around affirmation and negation, as one might say: "Carrying twenty exhausts so-and-so, nay, even ten," and the reverse would not be correct. Rather, what is intended is the correctness of the progression, which varies according to the context. Since being overtaken by sleep is more powerful in its effect and more detrimental to "All-Sustaining" (القيومية) than slumber, the requirement was to negate the effect and overtaking of slumber first, then progress to negating the effect of that which is more powerful in its effect. The meaning of "Neither slumber nor sleep overtakes Him" returns to something like our saying: "Neither this weak factor affects Him with languor in His affair, nor that which is stronger than it."

**His saying, the Exalted:** له ما في السماوات وما في الارض من ذا الذي يشفع عنده إِلا بإِذنه (To Him belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is on the earth. Who is it that may intercede with Him except by His permission?). Since the complete All-Sustaining (القيومية) belonging to Him, the Exalted, is not perfected except by His owning the heavens and the earth and what is in them with the reality of ownership (الملك), He mentioned it after them; just as complete monotheism (التوحيد) in divinity (الألوهية) is not perfected except by All-Sustaining, which is why He appended it to it as well.

These are two sentences, each of which is restricted, or as if restricted, by a condition in the sense of repelling an unspoken objection (دفع الدخل). I mean His saying, the Exalted: له ما في السماوات وما في الارض, along with His saying: من ذا الذي يشفع عنده إِلا بإِذنه; and His saying: يعلم ما بين أيديهم وما خلفهم, along with His saying: ولا يحيطون بشيء من علمه إِلا بما شاء.

As for His saying, the Exalted: له ما في السماوات وما في الارض, you have already learned the meaning of His ownership (الملك) of existing things and His sovereignty (الملك) over them. Ownership (الملك)—with a *kasrah* on the *mim*—is the subsistence of the essences of existing things and their attendant attributes and effects through Allah, the Glorified. This is what is indicated by His saying: له ما في السماوات وما في الارض. Thus, the sentence denotes ownership of the essence and the system of effects that follow the essence.

It has been established by His saying: القيوم لا تأخذه سنة ولا نوم له ما في السماوات وما في الارض that absolute authority (السلطان المطلق) in existence belongs to Allah, the Glorified; there is no disposal (تصرف) except that it belongs to Him and is from Him. From this, an illusion may arise in the imagination: if the matter is so, then what is the status of these existing causes and means (الاسباب والعلل) in the world? And how can influence (التأثير) be conceived in them and from them when there is no influence except for Allah, the Glorified?

The answer is that the disposal of these causes and means in these caused effects (الموجودات المعلولة) is a mediation (توسط) in disposal. In other words, it is intercession (شفاعة) in the instances of the effects (المسببات) by the permission of Allah, the Glorified. They are merely intercessors. Intercession (الشفاعة)—which is a kind of mediation in conveying good or repelling evil, and some disposal by the intercessor in the affair of the one seeking intercession—only contradicts divine authority and absolute Lordly disposal if it does not terminate in Allah's permission and does not rely on the will of Allah, the Exalted, but is instead independent and disconnected. There is no cause among the causes, nor any means among the means, except that its influence is through Allah and its mode of disposal is by Allah's permission. Thus, its influence and disposal are a mode of His influence and disposal, the Exalted. Therefore, there is no authority in existence except His authority, and no All-Sustaining except His absolute All-Sustaining, exalted is His authority.

Based on what we have explained, intercession is absolute mediation (التوسط المطلق) in the world of causes and intermediaries, encompassing both existential intercession (الشفاعة التكوينية)—which is the mediation of causes in creation—and legislative intercession (الشفاعة التشريعية)—meaning mediation in the stage of recompense, which the Book and the Sunnah establish for the Day of Resurrection, as previously discussed under His saying, the Exalted: **وَاتَّقُوا يَوْمًا لَّا تَجْزِي نَفْسٌ عَن نَّفْسٍ شَيْئًا** — "And beware of the day when no soul shall be of any avail to another soul" (al-Baqarah: 48). This is because the sentence, namely His saying, the Exalted: من ذا الذي يشفع عنده, is preceded by the discourse on All-Sustaining and absolute ownership, both of which encompass creation (التكوين) and legislation (التشريع) together; indeed, they are ostensibly concerned with creation. Thus, there is no reason to restrict them to legislative All-Sustaining and authority such that the conclusion of the speech would only apply to the specific intercession of the Day of Resurrection.

The course of this verse regarding the generality of intercession is like the course of His saying, the Exalted: **إِنَّ رَبَّكُمُ اللَّهُ الَّذِي خَلَقَ السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضَ فِي سِتَّةِ أَيَّامٍ ثُمَّ اسْتَوَىٰ عَلَى الْعَرْشِ يُدَبِّرُ الْأَمْرَ مَا مِن شَفِيعٍ إِلَّا مِن بَعْدِ إِذْنِهِ** — "Indeed your Lord is Allah, who created the heavens and the earth in six days, and then settled on the Throne, directing the command. There is no intercessor, except by His leave" (Yunus: 3). And His saying, the Exalted: **اللَّهُ الَّذِي خَلَقَ السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضَ وَمَا بَيْنَهُمَا فِي سِتَّةِ أَيَّامٍ ثُمَّ اسْتَوَىٰ عَلَى الْعَرْشِ مَا لَكُم مِّن دُونِهِ مِن وَلِيٍّ وَلَا شَفِيعٍ** — "It is Allah who created the heavens and the earth and whatever is between them in six days, then He settled on the Throne. You do not have besides Him any guardian or intercessor" (al-Sajdah: 4). You have already learned in the discussion on intercession (الشفاعة) — *the act of mediating on behalf of others before Allah* — that its definition applies to legislative intercession just as it applies to existential causality (السببية التكوينية). Every cause among the causes intercedes with Allah for its effect by clinging to the attributes of His grace, bounty, and mercy to convey the blessing of existence to its effect. Thus, the system of causality itself corresponds to the system of intercession, just as it corresponds to the system of supplication and asking. He, the Exalted, said: **يَسْأَلُهُ مَن فِي السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضِ كُلَّ يَوْمٍ هُوَ فِي شَأْنٍ** — "Everyone in the heavens and the earth asks Him. Every day He is engaged in some work" (al-Rahman: 29). And He, the Exalted, said: **وَآتَاكُم مِّن كُلِّ مَا سَأَلْتُمُوهُ** — "and He gave you all that you had asked Him" (Ibrahim: 34). This was previously explained in the commentary on His saying, the Exalted: **وَإِذَا سَأَلَكَ عِبَادِي عَنِّي** — "When My servants ask you about Me" (al-Baqarah: 186).

**His saying, the Exalted:** يعلم ما بين ايديهم وما خلفهم ولا يحيطون بشيء من علمه الا بما شاء (He knows what is before them and what is behind them, and they do not comprehend anything of His knowledge except what He wishes). The context of this sentence, being preceded by the matter of intercession, is close to the context of His saying, the Exalted: **بَلْ عِبَادٌ مُّكْرَمُونَ لَا يَسْبِقُونَهُ بِالْقَوْلِ وَهُم بِأَمْرِهِ يَعْمَلُونَ يَعْلَمُ مَا بَيْنَ أَيْدِيهِمْ وَمَا خَلْفَهُمْ وَلَا يَشْفَعُونَ إِلَّا لِمَنِ ارْتَضَىٰ وَهُم مِّنْ خَشْيَتِهِ مُشْفِقُونَ** — "Rather they are [His] honored servants. They do not venture to speak ahead of Him, and they act by His command. He knows what is before them and what is behind them, and they do not intercede except for him whom He approves, and they are apprehensive for fear of Him" (al-Anbiya: 26-28). It appears that the third-person plural pronoun refers to the intercessors (الشفعاء) indicated by the meaning of the previous sentence. Thus, His knowledge of what is before them and what is behind them is a metaphor for His complete encompassment (إحاطته) of them. They are unable, through this permitted intercession and mediation, to execute a matter that Allah, the Glorified, does not intend or approve of in His kingdom. Nor can anyone else benefit in an evil way from their intercession and mediation to interfere in His kingdom and do therein what He has not decreed.

A similar meaning is indicated by His saying, the Exalted: **وَمَا نَتَنَزَّلُ إِلَّا بِأَمْرِ رَبِّكَ لَهُ مَا بَيْنَ أَيْدِينَا وَمَا خَلْفَنَا وَمَا بَيْنَ ذَٰلِكَ وَمَا كَانَ رَبُّكَ نَسِيًّا** — "We do not descend except by the command of your Lord. To Him belongs whatever is before us and whatever is behind us and whatever is in between that, and your Lord is not forgetful" (Maryam: 64). And His saying, the Exalted: **عَالِمُ الْغَيْبِ فَلَا يُظْهِرُ عَلَىٰ غَيْبِهِ أَحَدًا إِلَّا مَنِ ارْتَضَىٰ مِن رَّسُولٍ فَإِنَّهُ يَسْلُكُ مِن بَيْنِ يَدَيْهِ وَمِنْ خَلْفِهِ رَصَدًا لِّيَعْلَمَ أَن قَدْ أَبْلَغُوا رِسَالَاتِ رَبِّهِمْ وَأَحَاطَ بِمَا لَدَيْهِمْ وَأَحْصَىٰ كُلَّ شَيْءٍ عَدَدًا** — "Knower of the Unseen, He does not disclose His Unseen to anyone, except to an apostle He approves of. Then He dispatches a sentinel before him and behind him, that He may know that they have delivered the messages of their Lord, and He encompasses all that is with them, and He keeps count of all things" (al-Jinn: 26-28). These verses explain His encompassment of the angels and prophets so that nothing occurs from them that He does not intend; they do not descend except by His command, and they do not deliver except what He wishes. Based on what we have explained, what is meant by "what is before them" (ما بين أيديهم) is what is present and witnessed with them, and by "what is behind them" (ما خلفهم) is what is absent from them and distant, such as their future state. The meaning thus resolves to the manifest (الشهادة) and the Unseen (الغيب).

In short, His saying: يعلم ما بين أيديهم وما خلفهم, is a metaphor for His encompassment of what is present with them and existing in their presence, and what is absent from them and coming behind them. Therefore, He followed it with His saying, the Exalted: ولا يحيطون بشيء من علمه إِلا بما شاء, to demonstrate the completeness of Lordly encompassment and divine authority; meaning that He is the Knower who encompasses them and their knowledge, while they do not encompass anything of His knowledge except what He wishes.

The referral of the masculine plural pronoun for rational beings (هم) in the three instances to the intercessors does not contradict what we previously stated—that intercession is more general than existential and legislative causality, and that the intercessors are all causes and means. This is because intercession, mediation, glorification (التسبيح), and praise (التحميد), being customarily recognized as acts of possessors of consciousness and intellect, are frequently expressed using terms specific to rational beings. This is the consistent method of the Quran in its explanations, such as His saying, the Exalted: **وَإِن مِّن شَيْءٍ إِلَّا يُسَبِّحُ بِحَمْدِهِ وَلَٰكِن لَّا تَفْقَهُونَ تَسْبِيحَهُمْ** — "There is not a thing but celebrates His praise, but you do not understand their glorification" (al-Isra: 44). And His saying, the Exalted: **ثُمَّ اسْتَوَىٰ إِلَى السَّمَاءِ وَهِيَ دُخَانٌ فَقَالَ لَهَا وَلِلْأَرْضِ ائْتِيَا طَوْعًا أَوْ كَرْهًا قَالَتَا أَتَيْنَا طَائِعِينَ** — "Then He turned to the heaven, and it was smoke, and He said to it and to the earth, ‘Come! willingly or unwillingly.’ They said, ‘We come willingly’" (Fussilat: 11), among other verses.

In short, His saying: ولا يحيطون بشيىء من علمه إِلا بما شاء, signifies the completeness and perfection of management (التدبير). For it is part of the perfection of management that the one being managed (المدبر) be unaware of what the manager (المدبر) intends for his state and future affair, lest he attempt to escape what he dislikes of the management and thereby spoil the manager's plan. It is like a group being moved against their desire and intent; the manager goes to great lengths to obscure things from them so they do not know from where they were moved, where they have landed, or where they are being taken.

Thus, the Exalted explains in this sentence that management belongs to Him and is by His knowledge of the connections between things, of which He is the placer. The rest of the causes and means—especially those possessing knowledge—even if they have disposal and knowledge, the knowledge they possess and benefit from is only from His knowledge, the Exalted, and by His wish and will. It is thus one of the affairs of divine knowledge, and their disposal is one of the affairs of divine disposal and the modes of His management. Therefore, it is not possible for any of them to proceed contrary to the management Allah, the Glorified, intends for His kingdom, except that it is itself part of that management.

In His saying, the Exalted: ولا يحيطون بشيء من علمه إِلا بما شاء—assuming that "knowledge" (العلم) refers to the infinitive meaning (المعنى المصدري) or the result of the infinitive (اسم المصدر) rather than the "known" (المعلوم)—there is an indication that all knowledge belongs to Allah, and no knowledge exists with any knower except that it is something of His knowledge, the Exalted. This is similar to what appears in His speech regarding the exclusivity of power (القدرة), might (العزة), and life (الحياة) to Allah. He, the Exalted, said: **وَلَوْ يَرَى الَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا إِذْ يَرَوْنَ الْعَدَابَ أَنَّ الْقُوَّةَ لِلَّهِ جَمِيعًا** — "That if the wrongdoers were to see—when they face the punishment—that power belongs entirely to Allah" (al-Baqarah: 165). And He, the Exalted, said: **أَيَبْتَغُونَ عِندَهُمُ الْعِزَّةَ فإِن الْعِزَّةَ لِلَّهِ جَمِيعًا** — "Do they seek honour with them? Indeed all honour belongs to Allah" (al-Nisa: 139). And He, the Exalted, said: **هُوَ الْحَيُّ لَا إِلَٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ** — "He is the Living One, there is no god except Him" (al-Mu’min: 65). It is possible to argue for the restriction of knowledge to Allah, the Exalted, by His saying: **إِنَّهُ هُوَ الْعَلِيمُ الْحَكِيمُ** — "Indeed He is the All-knowing, the All-wise" (Yusuf: 83), and His saying, the Exalted: **وَاللَّهُ يَعْلَمُ وَأَنتُمْ لَا تَعْلَمُونَ** — "and Allah knows and you do not know" (Al 'Imran: 66), among other verses. In the substitution of "knowledge" with "encompassment" (الإحاطة) in His saying: ولا يحيطون بشيء من علمه, there is a manifest subtlety.

**His saying, the Exalted:** وسع كرسيه السموات والارض (His Seat embraces the heavens and the earth). The Seat (الكرسي) is well-known, and it is so named because its parts are layered upon one another through craftsmanship. Sometimes the Seat is used as a metaphor for sovereignty (الملك); thus, one speaks of "the king's seat," meaning his sphere of influence and the extent of his power.

In any case, the sentences preceding this one—namely His saying: له ما في السموات وما في الارض etc.—indicate that what is meant by the embrace (سعة) of the Seat is the encompassment of the station of divine authority. Thus, the meaning of the Seat is determined to be: the Lordly station (المقام الربوبي) by which whatever is in the heavens and the earth subsists, insofar as they are owned, managed, and known. It is therefore one of the levels of knowledge. The meaning of "embrace" (السعة) is determined to be: the preservation of everything in the heavens and the earth in its essence and its effects. For this reason, He concluded it with His saying: ولا يؤده حفظهما.

**His saying, the Exalted:** ولا يؤده حفظهما وهو العلي العظيم (and their preservation does not tire Him; and He is the All-exalted, the All-supreme). It is said: *adahu ya'uduhu awdan* (آده يؤده أوداً) when something is heavy upon him, exhausts him, or tires him. It appears that the pronoun in *ya'uduhu* refers to the Seat, although it is permissible for it to refer to Him, the Exalted. The negation of fatigue and exhaustion from the preservation of the heavens and the earth at the end of the discourse is to correspond with what it opened with—the negation of slumber and sleep in the All-Sustaining of what is in the heavens and the earth.

The sum of the meaning conveyed by the verse is: Allah, there is no god except Him; to Him belongs all life and absolute All-Sustaining without weakness or languor. Therefore, the reasoning was concluded with the two noble names: the All-exalted (العلي) and the All-supreme (العظيم). For He, the Exalted, due to His exaltation, cannot be reached by the hands of creatures such that they might cause weakness in His existence or languor in His affair; and due to His supremacy, the multiplicity of creation does not exhaust Him, nor does the greatness of the heavens and the earth overwhelm Him. The sentence: وهو العلي العظيم, is not devoid of indicating restriction (الحصر). This restriction is either real—as is the truth, for exaltation and supremacy are perfections, and the reality of every perfection belongs to Him, the Exalted—or it is a claim necessitated by the context of reasoning, so that exaltation and supremacy are exclusively His by way of claim, whereby the heavens and the earth fall away from exaltation and supremacy in the face of His exaltation and supremacy, the Exalted.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In the *Tafsir* of al-Ayyashi, narrated from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him), who said: Abu Dharr said: "O Messenger of Allah, what is the best of what has been revealed to you?" He said: "The Verse of the Seat (آية الكرسي). The seven heavens and the seven earths are nothing in the Seat except like a ring cast in a desert land." Then he said: "And the excellence of the Throne (العرش) over the Seat is like the excellence of the desert over the ring."

**I say:** The beginning of the narration was recorded by al-Suyuti in *al-Durr al-Manthur* from Ibn Rahwayh in his *Musnad* from Awf ibn Malik from Abu Dharr. It was also narrated by Ahmad, Ibn al-Daris, al-Hakim—who authenticated it—and al-Bayhaqi in *Shu'ab al-Iman* from Abu Dharr.

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Ahmad and al-Tabarani narrated from Abu Umamah, who said: I said: "O Messenger of Allah, which [verse] revealed to you is the greatest?" He said: "Allah! There is no god except Him, the Living, the All-Sustainer—the Verse of the Seat."

**I say:** This meaning was also narrated therein from al-Khatib al-Baghdadi in his *Tarikh* from Anas from him (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him and his family).

Also therein, from al-Darimi from Ayfa' ibn Abdillah al-Kala'i, who said: A man said: "O Messenger of Allah, which verse in the Book of Allah is the greatest?" He said: "The Verse of the Seat: Allah! There is no god except Him, the Living, the All-Sustainer..." the *hadith*.

**I say:** The naming of this verse as "the Verse of the Seat" (آية الكرسي) was famous since the beginning of Islam, even during the lifetime of the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him and his family), and even on his tongue, as indicated by the narrations transmitted from him, from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (peace be upon them), and from the Companions. This is only due to the complete attention given to it and the magnification of its status, which stems from the nobility of the meaning it denotes, its subtlety, and its grace. It is pure monotheism (التوحيد) — *the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts* — denoted by His saying: الله لا إِله إِلا هو, and the meaning of absolute All-Sustaining (القيومية) to which all the Beautiful Names return, except for the Names of the Essence, as previously explained. It details the operation of All-Sustaining in all existing things, from the most subtle to the most magnificent, from their beginning to their end, by explaining that whatever appears to be outside of divine authority is, by virtue of being outside it, actually within it. For this reason, it has been narrated that it is the greatest verse in the Book of Allah. This is so because it encompasses a detailed explanation. For instance, His saying, the Exalted: **اللَّهُ لَا إِلَٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ لَهُ الْأَسْمَاءُ الْحُسْنَىٰ** — "Allah—there is no god except Him—to Him belong the Best Names" (Taha: 8), although it encompasses what the Verse of the Seat encompasses, it does so in a summary fashion without detail. Thus, it is mentioned in some reports that the Verse of the Seat is the "mistress" (سيدة) of the verses of the Quran, as narrated in *al-Durr al-Manthur* from Abu Hurayrah from the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him and his family). And it is mentioned in some that for everything there is a peak (ذروة), and the peak of the Quran is the Verse of the Seat, as narrated by al-Ayyashi in his *Tafsir* from Abdillah ibn Sinan from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him).

In the *Amali* of the Shaykh, with his chain of narration from Abu Umamah al-Bahili: that he heard Ali ibn Abi Talib (peace be upon him) saying: "I do not see a man whose intellect has grasped Islam, or who was born in Islam, spending a night of its darkness..." I said: "What is its darkness?" He said: "All of it, until he recites this verse: الله لا إِله إِلا هو الحي القيوم." He then recited the verse until His saying: ولا يؤده حفظهما وهو العلي العظيم. He said: "If you knew what it is—or he said: what is in it—you would not leave it under any circumstance. Indeed, the Messenger of Allah (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him and his family) said: 'I was given the Verse of the Seat from a treasure beneath the Throne, and no prophet before me was given it.'" Ali said: "I have not spent a single night since I heard it from the Messenger of Allah except that I recited it," the *hadith*.

**I say:** This meaning was narrated in *al-Durr al-Manthur* from Ubayd, Ibn Abi Shaybah, al-Darimi, Muhammad ibn Nasr, and Ibn al-Daris from him (peace be upon him). It was also narrated from al-Daylami from him (peace be upon him). The narrations from the paths of the Shi'a and the Ahl al-Sunnah regarding its merit are numerous. His saying (peace be upon him): "Indeed, the Messenger of Allah said: 'I was given the Verse of the Seat from a treasure beneath the Throne,'" was also narrated in this sense in *al-Durr al-Manthur* from al-Bukhari in his *Tarikh* and Ibn al-Daris from Anas, that the Prophet said: "I was given the Verse of the Seat from beneath the Throne." In this is an indication that the Seat is beneath the Throne and encompassed by it, and the discussion regarding its explanation will follow.

In *al-Kafi*, from Zurarah, who said: I asked Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) about the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: وسع كرسيه السماوات والارض. "Did the heavens and the earth embrace the Seat, or did the Seat embrace the heavens and the earth?" He (peace be upon him) replied: "Indeed, everything is in the Seat."

**I say:** This meaning is narrated from them in several reports with similar questions and answers. On its surface, it is strange, as no reading has been narrated with "His Seat" (كرسيه) in the accusative and "the heavens and the earth" in the nominative to justify such a question. It appears that it is based on what common understandings might imagine—that the Seat is a specific physical body placed above the heavens or the seventh heaven (meaning above the world of physical bodies) from which the decrees of the physical world originate. Thus, the heavens and the earth would be its embrace since it is placed upon them like the form of a chair on the ground. The meaning of the question would then be that it is more appropriate that the heavens and the earth embraced the Seat, so what is the meaning of its embrace of them? Something similar has been said regarding the Throne specifically, and the answer was that the embrace is not of the same kind as the embrace of some physical bodies by others.

In *al-Ma'ani*, from Hafs ibn al-Ghiyath, who said: I asked Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) about the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: وسع كرسيه السماوات والارض. He said: "His knowledge."

Also therein, from him (peace be upon him) regarding the verse: "The heavens and the earth and what is between them are in the Seat, and the Throne (العرش) is the knowledge that no one can measure."

**I say:** It appears from the two narrations that the Seat is one of the levels of His knowledge, the Exalted, as previously inferred. There are other narrations with the same meaning.

Likewise, it appears from them and from what will follow that in existence there is a level of knowledge that is unlimited; meaning that above this world, of which we are parts, there is another world whose existing things are not limited in their existence by these physical boundaries and existential determinations (التعينات الوجودية) that our existences have. While being unlimited, they are known to Allah, the Glorified; meaning their existence is the very essence of knowledge. Just as the limited existing things in existence are known to Allah, the Glorified, in the level of their existence—meaning their existence is His very knowledge of them and their presence with Him. Perhaps we will be granted the success to explain this knowledge, called "active knowledge" (العلم الفعلي), in what follows regarding His saying, the Exalted: **وَمَا يَعْزُبُ عَن رَّبِّكَ مِن مِّثْقَالِ ذَرَّةٍ فِي الْأَرْضِ** — "Not an atom’s weight escapes your Lord in the earth" (Yunus: 61).

What we mentioned regarding unlimited knowledge is what is pointed to by his saying (peace be upon him) in the narration: "and the Throne is the knowledge that no one can measure." It is well-known that the lack of measure and limitation is not due to the numerical abundance of the objects of this knowledge, for the existence of an infinite number is impossible; every number that enters existence is finite, being less than what exceeds it by one. If the infinity of knowledge—meaning the Throne—were due to the numerical infinity of its objects, the Seat would be part of the Throne since it is also knowledge, even if limited. Rather, the lack of infinity and measure is from the aspect of the perfection of existence (كمال الوجود); meaning that existential boundaries and restrictions necessitate multiplicity, distinction, and differentiation among the existing things of our material world. They necessitate the division of species into types and individuals, and individuals into states and relations that do not exist [there]. Thus, it corresponds to His saying, the Exalted: **وَإِن مِّن شَيْءٍ إِلَّا عِندَنَا خَزَائِنُهُ وَمَا نُنَزِّلُهُ إِلَّا بِقَدَرٍ مَّعْلُومٍ** — "There is not a thing but its treasuries are with Us, and We do not send it down except in a known measure" (al-Hijr: 21). The full discussion on this will follow, if Allah wills.

These existing things, just as they are known with an unmeasured knowledge—meaning existing in the vessel of knowledge with an unmeasured existence—they are likewise known with their boundaries, existing in the vessel of knowledge with their measures. This is the Seat (الكرسي), according to what is inferred.

Perhaps His saying, the Exalted, therein also alludes to this: يعلم ما بين أيديهم وما خلفهم, where He made the known: "what is before them and what is behind them." These two—namely what is before and what is behind—do not coexist in existence in this material world. Thus, there is a station where all temporal and similar dispersions are gathered. These existences are not existences of infinite perfection, unlimited and unmeasured; otherwise, the exception from encompassment in His saying, the Exalted: ولا يحيطون بشيء من علمه إِلا بما شاء, would not be correct. Therefore, it is inevitably a station where it is possible for them to encompass some of what is in it. It is thus the stage of knowledge of limited and measured things insofar as they are limited and measured. Allah knows best.

In *al-Tawhid*, from Hannan, who said: I asked Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) about the Throne and the Seat. He (peace be upon him) said: "Indeed, the Throne has many different attributes; for every cause and act in the Quran, it has a separate attribute. Thus, His saying: 'Lord of the Great Throne' (رب العرش العظيم) means: Lord of the great kingdom. And His saying: 'The All-beneficent, settled on the Throne' (الرحمن على العرش استوى) means: He encompassed the kingdom. This is the knowledge of the 'howness' (الكيفوفية) in things. Furthermore, the Throne, when joined [with the Seat], is distinct from the Seat, because they are two of the greatest gates of the Unseen (الغيوب), and both are Unseen. They are joined in the Unseen because the Seat is the manifest gate of the Unseen from which the 'innovations' (البدع) emerge and from which all things come. The Throne is the hidden gate in which is found the knowledge of the how, the being, the measure, the limit, the wish, the attribute of the will, the knowledge of words, movements, and cessation, and the knowledge of the return and the beginning. Thus, they are two joined gates in knowledge, because the kingdom of the Throne is other than the kingdom of the Seat, and its knowledge is more hidden than the knowledge of the Seat. Therefore, He said: 'Lord of the Great Throne,' meaning its attribute is greater than the attribute of the Seat, and in that they are joined." I said: "May I be your ransom, why did it [the Throne] become the neighbor of the Seat in excellence?" He (peace be upon him) said: "It became its neighbor because the knowledge of the 'howness' is in it, and in it are the manifest gates of 'abeyance' (البداء) and its 'is-ness' (إنيتها), and the limit of its joining and separating. These are two neighbors, one of which carries its companion in the vessel, and by such [examples] the scholars explain, so that they may prove the truth of their claim, for 'He singles out for His mercy whomever He wishes, and He is the All-mighty, the All-wise.'"

**I say:** His saying (peace be upon him): "because the Seat is the manifest gate of the Unseen," you have already learned the reason for this in summary. The level of measured and limited knowledge is closer to our measured and limited physical world than that which has no measure or limit. The explanation of the segments of the narration will follow in the discussion on His saying, the Exalted: **إِنَّ رَبَّكُمُ اللَّهُ الَّذِي خَلَقَ السَّمَاوَاتِ** — "Indeed your Lord is Allah, who created the heavens" (al-A'raf: 54). His saying (peace be upon him): "and by such [examples] the scholars explain," is an indication that these terms—the Throne, the Seat, and their likes—are parables coined and struck for people, and none understand them except the learned.

In *al-Ihtijaj*, from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him), in a *hadith*: "Everything Allah created is in the hollow of the Seat except His Throne, for it is too great for the Seat to encompass it."

**I say:** The clarification of its meaning has preceded, and it is in agreement with other narrations. As for what occurred in some reports—that the Throne is the knowledge that Allah disclosed to His prophets and messengers, and the Seat is the knowledge that He did not disclose to anyone, as narrated by al-Saduq from al-Mufaddal from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him)—it seems to be an error by the narrator in swapping the positions of the two terms, namely the Throne and the Seat, or it is to be discarded like the narration attributed to Zaynab al-Attarah.

In the *Tafsir* of al-Ayyashi, from Ali (peace be upon him), who said: "Indeed, the heaven and the earth and what is between them of created creation are in the hollow of the Seat, and it has four angels who carry it by the command of Allah."

**I say:** Al-Saduq narrated it from al-Asbagh ibn Nubatah from him (peace be upon him). No carriers (حملة) for the Seat have been narrated from them (peace be upon them) except in this narration. Rather, the reports only establish carriers for the Throne in accordance with the Book of Allah, as He said: **الَّذِينَ يَحْمِلُونَ الْعَرْشَ وَمَنْ حَوْلَهُ** — "Those who bear the Throne and those around it..." (al-Mu’min: 7). And He, the Exalted, said: **وَيَحْمِلُ عَرْشَ رَبِّكَ فَوْقَهُمْ يَوْمَئِذٍ ثَمَانِيَةٌ** — "and eight [angels] will bear that day the Throne of your Lord above them" (al-Haqqah: 17). It is possible to reconcile the report by noting that the Seat—as will be explained—is united with the Throne in a way, like the union of the manifest of a thing with its hidden. Thereby, it becomes correct to count the carriers of one as the carriers of the other.

In the *Tafsir* of al-Ayyashi also, from Mu'awiyah ibn Ammar from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him), who said: I said: "Who is it that may intercede with Him except by His permission?" He said: "We are those intercessors (الشافعون)."

**I say:** Al-Barqi also narrated it in *al-Mahasin*. You have already learned that intercession in the verse is absolute, encompassing both existential and legislative intercession together; thus, it encompasses their intercession (peace be upon them). The narration is of the nature of "application" (الجري).
